natural_occurrence.description: An aromatic carboxylic acid that occurs naturally in many plants. First isolated from gum benzoin resin in the 16th century, hence its name. While naturally present in many fruits and spices, commercial benzoic acid is produced synthetically.

manufacturing.description: Industrially produced by partial oxidation of toluene with oxygen in the presence of metal catalysts (typically cobalt or manganese compounds). Can also be produced by hydrolysis of benzotrichloride or through the decarboxylation of phthalic acid.

other_uses.cosmetics: Used as a preservative in cosmetics and personal care products
other_uses.industrial: Used in the production of phenol, caprolactam, benzoyl chloride, and plasticizers
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Used as a preservative in liquid medicines; intermediate in drug synthesis

eu.conditions: Maximum levels typically 150-500 mg/kg; concern about benzene formation when combined with ascorbic acid

usa.notes: Maximum usage 0.1%; FDA issued guidance on benzene formation in beverages in 2006

japan.notes: Use limited to caviar, margarine, soft drinks, syrup, soy sauce, and fruit paste in confectionery

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: 0–5 mg/kg bw/day (group ADI for benzoic acid and benzoates)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: 5 mg/kg bw/day (group ADI for benzoic acid and benzoates)

regulatory_history.event: Benzoic acid first described by Nostradamus, distilled from gum benzoin
First synthesized from toluene by Friedrich Wöhler
FDA issues guidance on benzene formation in beverages containing both benzoic acid/benzoates and ascorbic acid
JECFA and EFSA confirm group ADI of 5 mg/kg bw/day for benzoic acid and benzoates

adi_context.source_note: Based on JECFA/EFSA group ADI for benzoic acid and benzoates. Benzoic acid is less commonly used than its sodium salt (E211) due to lower water solubility. Concern exists about benzene formation when combined with vitamin C in acidic beverages.
